title: Alcohol Withdrawal Syndrome
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Internal Medicine, Addiction Medicine
objectives: Recognize spectrum of alcohol withdrawal from mild to DTs
Apply CIWA-Ar protocol for symptom-triggered therapy

vignette.text:
A 54-year-old man presents with tremors, anxiety, and diaphoresis. He reports drinking a pint of vodka daily for years but stopped 36 hours ago when hospitalized for pneumonia. He is now increasingly agitated.

Past Medical History: Alcohol use disorder, prior withdrawal seizure 2 years ago

Medications: None

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.neuro: Alert, oriented, coarse tremor, no focal deficits, no hallucinations currently

questions.stem: This patient has tremors, anxiety, and autonomic hyperactivity 36 hours after last drink.
questions.question: What is the spectrum and timeline of alcohol withdrawal?

options.text: DTs occur within 12 hours
Minor withdrawal starts after 48 hours
Seizures occur after 72 hours

ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL TIMELINE: MINOR WITHDRAWAL (6-24 hours): Tremor, anxiety, insomnia; Diaphoresis, palpitations; Anorexia, nausea; WITHDRAWAL SEIZURES (12-48 hours): Brief, generalized tonic-clonic; Usually single or few; Risk of status epilepticus ~3%; ALCOHOLIC HALLUCINOSIS (12-48 hours): Visual, auditory, or tactile hallucinations; INTACT sensorium (oriented); Resolves in 24-48 hours; DELIRIUM TREMENS (48-96 hours): Peak 72-96 hours; Severe autonomic instability; Profound confusion/agitation; Hallucinations with altered sensorium; Mortality 5-15% if untreated; RISK FACTORS FOR SEVERE AWS: Prior severe withdrawal/DTs; Prior withdrawal seizures; Heavy/prolonged drinking; Concurrent illness; Older age; Abnormal LFTs

questions.explanation: Alcohol Withdrawal Timeline: MINOR (6-24h): Tremor, anxiety, autonomic signs. SEIZURES (12-48h): Brief, generalized, usually single. HALLUCINOSIS (12-48h): Hallucinations WITH intact sensorium. DELIRIUM TREMENS (48-96h, peak 72-96h): Confusion, severe autonomic instability, hallucinations, 5-15% mortality untreated. RISK FACTORS: Prior severe withdrawal/DTs/seizures, heavy drinking, concurrent illness, older age.

sources.text: StatPearls: Alcohol Withdrawal. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to assess severity of withdrawal.
questions.question: How is alcohol withdrawal severity assessed?

options.text: Score <20 is severe
CIWA-Ar works in altered patients

CIWA-Ar SCALE (0-67 points): 10 items scored 0-7: Nausea/vomiting; Tremor; Paroxysmal sweats; Anxiety; Agitation; Tactile disturbances; Auditory disturbances; Visual disturbances; Headache; Orientation/clouding; INTERPRETATION: <10: Mild (may not need pharmacotherapy); 10-18: Moderate; >18: Severe (high risk for complications); LIMITATIONS: Requires patient cooperation; Not valid if intubated/sedated; Can't use with altered mental status; ALTERNATIVES: PAWSS: Prediction of AWS Severity; SHOT scale; Clinical judgment + risk factors; This patient: Tremor + anxiety + diaphoresis + tachycardia + hypertension + prior seizure = HIGH RISK
CIWA-Ar is scored 0-100

questions.explanation: CIWA-Ar Scale (0-67 points): 10 items including tremor, anxiety, sweats, agitation, hallucinations, orientation. INTERPRETATION: <10 mild, 10-18 moderate, >18 severe. LIMITATIONS: Requires cooperation, not valid if altered/intubated. This patient has multiple symptoms plus prior seizure = HIGH RISK despite possibly moderate CIWA.

sources.text: StatPearls: Alcohol Withdrawal. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the pharmacological management of alcohol withdrawal?

options.text: Fixed-schedule always preferred

ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL TREATMENT: BENZODIAZEPINES (first-line): SYMPTOM-TRIGGERED (preferred if CIWA can be used): Give BZD when CIWA ≥10; Reassess q1h; Less medication, shorter duration; FIXED-SCHEDULE: When CIWA unreliable; Scheduled doses + PRN; FRONT-LOADING: For severe withdrawal; Diazepam 10-20mg q1-2h until calm; Then taper or switch to PRN; AGENT SELECTION: Diazepam: Long-acting, self-tapering, smooth; Lorazepam: Hepatic impairment, no active metabolites; Chlordiazepoxide: Traditional, long-acting; ADJUNCTS: Phenobarbital: BZD-resistant withdrawal; Dexmedetomidine: ICU, reduces BZD needs; Propofol: Refractory DTs; AVOID: Beta-blockers alone (mask, don't treat); Antipsychotics alone (lower seizure threshold)
Beta-blockers are first-line
Antipsychotics prevent seizures

questions.explanation: AWS Treatment: BENZODIAZEPINES first-line. SYMPTOM-TRIGGERED preferred when CIWA reliable (give when ≥10). FIXED-SCHEDULE when CIWA unreliable. FRONT-LOADING for severe withdrawal. AGENTS: Diazepam (long-acting, self-tapering), Lorazepam (hepatic impairment). ADJUNCTS: Phenobarbital for BZD-resistant, dexmedetomidine. AVOID beta-blockers alone (mask symptoms), antipsychotics alone (lower seizure threshold).

sources.text: StatPearls: Alcohol Withdrawal. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient has a prior withdrawal seizure.
questions.question: How should withdrawal seizures be managed?

options.text: Phenytoin prevents withdrawal seizures

WITHDRAWAL SEIZURE MANAGEMENT: ACUTE SEIZURE: Benzodiazepines (lorazepam 2-4mg IV); Most are self-limited; Status epilepticus rare (~3%); PREVENTION: Treat underlying withdrawal adequately; Benzodiazepines prevent seizures; Prior seizure = high risk for recurrence; AFTER SEIZURE: Continue/escalate BZD therapy; Rule out other causes (trauma, infection, metabolic); DO NOT need long-term AEDs for isolated withdrawal seizure; Watch for progression to DTs (can occur 24-48h after seizure); PHENYTOIN: NOT effective for AWS seizures (different mechanism); Do not use for prophylaxis; This patient: Prior seizure + current moderate withdrawal = needs aggressive BZD treatment
Long-term AEDs needed after withdrawal seizure
Seizures mean DTs won't occur

questions.explanation: Withdrawal Seizure Management: ACUTE: Benzodiazepines (lorazepam 2-4mg IV), most self-limited. PREVENTION: Adequate BZD treatment, prior seizure increases risk. AFTER: Continue/escalate BZDs, rule out other causes, no long-term AEDs needed for isolated withdrawal seizures. PHENYTOIN is NOT effective for AWS seizures. Watch for DTs after seizure (can still occur).

sources.text: StatPearls: Alcohol Withdrawal. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: How should thiamine deficiency be addressed?

options.text: THIAMINE IN ALCOHOL USE DISORDER: WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY TRIAD: Encephalopathy (confusion); Oculomotor dysfunction (nystagmus, ophthalmoplegia); Ataxia (gait disturbance); NOTE: Full triad in only ~10%!; TREATMENT: HIGH-DOSE IV THIAMINE: Suspected/confirmed Wernicke: 500mg IV TID × 2-3 days; Then 250mg IV daily × 3-5 days; Then oral; Prophylaxis (all at-risk patients): 100-250mg IV daily; CRITICAL: Give thiamine BEFORE glucose; Glucose without thiamine can precipitate Wernicke; KORSAKOFF SYNDROME: Chronic, irreversible memory disorder; Confabulation; Results from untreated Wernicke; DO NOT MISS WERNICKE: High index of suspicion; Low threshold to treat (safe); Irreversible if untreated
Full triad usually present in Wernicke
Give glucose before thiamine
Oral thiamine adequate for Wernicke

questions.explanation: Thiamine in AUD: WERNICKE TRIAD (only ~10% have all 3): Encephalopathy, oculomotor dysfunction, ataxia. TREATMENT: HIGH-DOSE IV - suspected Wernicke: 500mg IV TID × 2-3 days, then taper; Prophylaxis: 100-250mg IV daily. CRITICAL: Give thiamine BEFORE glucose (glucose can precipitate Wernicke). Untreated Wernicke → Korsakoff syndrome (irreversible memory disorder).

sources.text: StatPearls: Alcohol Withdrawal. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient develops confusion and severe agitation on day 3.
questions.question: How is delirium tremens managed?

options.text: DTs can be managed on floor
Mortality is <1% with treatment
Low-dose BZDs are sufficient

DELIRIUM TREMENS MANAGEMENT: RECOGNITION: Severe confusion/agitation; Hallucinations with altered sensorium; Severe autonomic instability; Onset 48-96h (peak 72-96h); ICU ADMISSION: Close monitoring; May need intubation; AGGRESSIVE BENZODIAZEPINES: High doses often required; Diazepam 10-20mg IV q5-10min until calm; Some patients need hundreds of mg; BZD-RESISTANT DTs: Phenobarbital 130-260mg IV q15-20min; Propofol infusion (requires intubation); Dexmedetomidine (adjunct, not monotherapy); SUPPORTIVE: IV fluids; Correct electrolytes (K+, Mg2+, Phos); Thiamine; Cooling if hyperthermic; MORTALITY: 5-15% even with treatment; Higher without recognition; From cardiovascular collapse, infection, trauma

questions.explanation: Delirium Tremens Management: ICU admission required. AGGRESSIVE BENZODIAZEPINES often in very high doses. BZD-RESISTANT: Phenobarbital, propofol (requires intubation). SUPPORTIVE: Fluids, electrolyte correction, thiamine. MORTALITY 5-15% even with treatment, from cardiovascular collapse, infection, or trauma. DTs peak at 72-96 hours.

sources.text: StatPearls: Alcohol Withdrawal. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Complete approach: (1) ASSESS RISK: Prior withdrawal seizure = HIGH RISK; Heavy daily drinking, 36h since last drink, moderate symptoms; (2) GIVE THIAMINE FIRST: 100-250mg IV before any glucose; (3) CIWA-Ar ASSESSMENT: Calculate score, monitor q1-2h initially; (4) TREAT AGGRESSIVELY given risk factors: Symptom-triggered BZDs (CIWA ≥10); Consider front-loading given prior seizure; Diazepam or lorazepam (lorazepam if hepatic); (5) CORRECT ELECTROLYTES: K+ 3.2 → replete; Mg2+ 1.4 → replete (often co-depleted); (6) MONITOR FOR PROGRESSION: Seizures (12-48h window); DTs (48-96h window); (7) SUPPORTIVE: Fluids, nutrition, treat pneumonia; (8) AVOID: Beta-blockers alone, antipsychotics alone, phenytoin; (9) PLAN FOR RECOVERY: Addiction medicine consult; Consider naltrexone/acamprosate on discharge
Low-dose BZDs adequate given only moderate symptoms
Phenytoin for seizure prophylaxis
Give glucose before thiamine

questions.explanation: Complete AWS Approach: ASSESS RISK (prior seizure = high risk). THIAMINE FIRST before glucose. CIWA assessment and monitoring. AGGRESSIVE BZD TREATMENT given risk factors - consider front-loading. CORRECT ELECTROLYTES (K+, Mg2+). MONITOR for seizures (12-48h) and DTs (48-96h). SUPPORTIVE care. AVOID beta-blockers alone, antipsychotics alone, phenytoin. PLAN for recovery with addiction medicine involvement.

sources.text: StatPearls: Alcohol Withdrawal. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

references.title: AACT Position Paper: Single-Dose Activated Charcoal

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected alcohol withdrawal?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in alcohol withdrawal. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of alcohol withdrawal. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing alcohol withdrawal, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents 2 hours after ingesting an unknown quantity of acetaminophen. Serum APAP level at 4 hours post-ingestion is 220 mcg/mL. Using the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, what is the appropriate management?

options.text: Start IV N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — the level is above the treatment line
options.explanation: On the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, the treatment line at 4 hours is 150 mcg/mL (FDA line) or 200 mcg/mL (original Rumack-Matthew line). At 220 mcg/mL, the level exceeds both thresholds. IV NAC (150 mg/kg loading, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h) should be started immediately.

options.text: Recheck level at 8 hours before deciding on treatment
options.explanation: With a clearly supratherapeutic level at 4 hours, treatment should begin immediately. Delaying NAC beyond 8 hours post-ingestion significantly increases risk of hepatotoxicity.

options.text: Administer activated charcoal only — NAC is not indicated within 4 hours of ingestion
options.explanation: Activated charcoal may be beneficial if given within 1-2 hours of ingestion, but it is NOT a substitute for NAC when the level indicates toxicity. NAC should be started as soon as possible.

options.text: Obtain LFTs and treat only if transaminases are elevated
options.explanation: Hepatotoxicity from acetaminophen is delayed (ALT typically rises at 24-72 hours). Normal LFTs at 4 hours do not exclude impending liver injury. The nomogram guides treatment BEFORE liver damage occurs.

sections.content: Systematic approach to Alcohol Withdrawal including recognition, diagnosis, and management.

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content: Evidence-based clinical pearls for Alcohol Withdrawal.
